responses.strategic_rationale: This 3-year residential parasomnia research unit faces a tripartite tension: operational safety and data fidelity demand extended in-situ monitoring, yet acquisition costs and recruitment constraints limit cohort scale; scientific rigor requires dual-rater annotation and semi-automated event detection, yet annotation throughput and technician burnout create a bottleneck; and institutional sustainability demands early publication and proof-of-concept, yet event capture is rare and unpredictable, risking 2-year lag before publishable findings. The five levers below address these core trade-offs by controlling (1) participant length-of-stay strategy and its yield-versus-cost trade-off, (2) sensor-suite composition balancing fidelity-versus-burden, (3) event-detection workflow ownership and manual-versus-automated review intensity, (4) facility staffing architecture under variable census, and (5) data-sharing scope and its publication-velocity-versus-privacy tension. Together they navigate the central project paradox: maximizing rare-event capture and scientific credibility within fixed 3-year funding and constrained recruitment.

levers.name: Participant Length-of-Stay Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Short stays (2 weeks) reduce per-participant cost and increase recruitment throughput, allowing 70+ enrollments in year one. Systemic: Extended stays (8+ weeks) capture lower-frequency events with 25–30% higher yield per participant but lock capital in fewer, longer admissions. Strategic: The choice determines whether the unit achieves Aim 2 longitudinal characterization via dense within-person sampling or via broader between-person heterogeneity mapping—with implications for publication timeline and algorithm generalizability.
options: Standardized 2-week stays for all participants regardless of event yield, with extension-on-demand only after zero-event protocol trigger, prioritizing recruitment volume and rapid turnover to reach 60+ enrollments by month 24
Variable stays structured as 4-week baseline for 80% of cohort plus 6–8 week extended protocols for participants with low event frequency or complex phenotypes, balancing yield and throughput

Adaptive-extension model: all participants default to 3-week stays; those with zero events by day 21 automatically extend to week 7 or 8 at reduced per-diem; those with ≥2 events exit after week 2 if participant-requested, preserving data while minimizing underutilized stays
levers.review_lever: Controls Recruitment Volume vs. Event Yield. Weakness: the options do not explicitly address the staffing surge required during cohort turnover (intake/discharge cycles every 2 weeks in the short-stay model), which may compromise safety oversight and annotation quality during peak census transitions.

levers.name: Sensor-Tiering and Data-Burden Trade-off
levers.consequences: Immediate: Low-burden nightly dry-EEG headbands and mattress sensors enable high compliance and naturalistic behavior preservation, capturing 90%+ of nights. Systemic: Reduced overnight technician time for event response, but contact-free modalities suffer 15–20% false-positive artifact rates requiring higher manual-review burden. Strategic: Escalation-night full PSG adds clinical-grade resolution at ~€400 per night and delays clinical decision-making; omitting it risks missing rare morphological details required for Aim 3 algorithm training.
options: Lean tiering: nightly dry-EEG and contactless sensors only, with no scheduled enhanced-night PSG; full PSG deployed solely on nights after technician-flagged events, reducing equipment burden and freeing ~€150K year-one capital but risking sparse high-resolution data for algorithm development

Standard three-tier model (nightly lightweight + scheduled enhanced PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, final) plus event-triggered escalation, providing balanced training data at planned cost, with trade-off of participant discomfort and technician setup time on enhanced nights

Symmetric full-tier model: continuous full PSG (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on all nights alongside contactless sensors, maximizing artifact detection and morphological granularity but increasing equipment cost by ~€200K year one, technician load, and participant burden to the point of suppressing naturalistic behavior
levers.review_lever: Controls Physiological Resolution vs. Operational Load. Weakness: none of the options address the unequal value of scheduled enhanced nights across participants—some may exhibit all events on odd nights, rendering mid-stay PSG non-contributory while missing critical episodes elsewhere.

levers.name: Event-Triage and Annotation Workflow Ownership
levers.consequences: Immediate: Technician real-time annotation during night shifts captures context and behavioral nuance but introduces fatigue bias and inconsistency. Systemic: Daytime dual-rater scoring ensures inter-rater reliability but 6–10 week annotation lag creates feedback delay to the technician, reducing quality control loop. Strategic: Automated pre-triage reduces manual burden by 30–40% but requires large training dataset (>500 events), pushing model maturity to month 20+, potentially delaying Aim 3 delivery.
options: Technician-centric: all real-time and next-day preliminary annotation performed by night technician and next-shift reviewer; daytime dual-rater scoring applied only to events flagged as borderline or novel, reducing dual-rater load by ~40% but risking systematic bias in what reaches expert review

Hybrid queue-based triage: technician flags potential events in real time; a statistical filter (e.g., movement amplitude + EEG arousal pattern thresholds) ranks flagged events by probability; daytime raters prioritize high-probability queue first, then exhaustively score lower-probability backlog, creating productivity tiering

Delayed-batch with algorithmic pre-screen: technician monitors safety only; all annotation deferred to daytime; candidate events automatically rank-ordered by a supervised-learning model trained on 100+ initial dual-scored nights; raters review model-ranked queue in priority order, completing Aim 3 algorithm concurrently with annotation
levers.review_lever: Controls Annotation-Throughput vs. Inter-Rater Reliability. Weakness: the options assume stable technician roster and dual-rater availability, but staff turnover (especially night technicians) will degrade real-time tagging consistency and daytime rater expertise unevenly across the 3-year period, yet none offer mitigation or recalibration protocols.

levers.name: Staffing Flexibility and Night-Coverage Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Fixed 3-technician roster covers 8 suites safely but offers no surge capacity when census approaches 12 suites or when technician absence occurs. Systemic: On-call rotation plus one additional part-time technician adds ~€80K year-two salary but enables 10–12 suite operation and buffers staff turnover; alternatively, outsourced overnight staffing (temp agency) reduces fixed cost but introduces inconsistency and delayed response. Strategic: Staffing determines maximum safe census, directly constraining enrollment rate and event yield; undersizing creates bottlenecks; oversizing in year one wastes budget before data quality is proven.
options: Core fixed roster: retain planned 3 full-time technicians for 8 active suites with strict no-flex admissions ceiling; defer 9–12 suite staffing to phase two; saves ~€200K year one but caps annual enrollment at ~35 participants and risks project failure if event yield is lower than predicted

Tiered flexible model: 3 core FTE technicians for 8 suites baseline; hire one additional 0.5 FTE night technician in year two (shared with daytime annotation) to enable 10-suite operation; implement structured on-call for emergencies without planned reliance, increasing flexibility while controlling cost escalation

Hybrid outsource-and-in-house: retain 2 core in-house technicians for continuity and training; contract external night-shift staffing for 50% of coverage (e.g., 3–4 nights per week from a vetted clinical staffing agency), reducing fixed overhead by ~€150K annually but risking protocol-adherence gaps and increased incident reporting burden
levers.review_lever: Controls Fixed Cost vs. Adaptive Capacity. Weakness: the options ignore the training and onboarding burden for external or part-time staff—overnight technicians require 4–6 weeks of competency building on the tiered sensor suite and safety protocols, yet none of the models front-load this time or cost explicitly.

levers.name: Data-Sharing Scope and Publication-Acceleration Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: De-identified physiological-only repository deposits (excluding video) enable rapid public archiving and third-party algorithm benchmarking by month 18, accelerating external validation and citation impact. Systemic: Limiting public data to sensor streams and scored-event annotations (not raw video) protects privacy but excludes qualitative behavioral context that enriches phenotyping; restricting sharing to signed data-use agreements delays deposit timeline by 6–12 weeks per request. Strategic: Delaying public data release until final report (month 36) maximizes the unit's publication window but risks parallel external work and reduces early impact; releasing mid-stream (month 18+) seeds the field but may expose methodological limitations before peer-review publication.
options: Early open-access deposit: physiological data and event annotations archived in a public BIDS-compliant repository (e.g., OpenNeuro) by month 16, concurrent with first-draft manuscript submission; video excluded; minimal data-use agreement overhead; prioritizes rapid field engagement and algorithm replication over proprietary advantage

Tiered staggered release: first interim dataset (cohort 1, n=25) with 12-month embargo under institutional control, released with accompanying methods paper at month 20; second dataset (cohort 2, n=25–40) at month 28; final cohort released month 36; balances publication priority with controlled dissemination and partner influence windows

Restricted access with consortium: physiological data available only to investigator-nominated collaborators under formal data-use agreements reviewed by PI and advisory board; video never shared; full dataset archive restricted to qualified sleep research institutions; maximizes confidentiality and negotiating leverage with pharmaceutical partners but delays external validation and risks replication challenges
levers.review_lever: Controls Publication Velocity vs. Privacy and Partnership Leverage. Weakness: the options treat video as uniformly excludable, yet some de-identified lower-body or hands-only video of motor phenomena (absent faces) could enrich phenotyping and algorithm training without severe privacy violation; none of the options explore granular video-segmentation or temporal-blur approaches that might unlock conditional sharing.

responses.summary: The five levers are well-balanced in structure and address the core trade-offs (volume vs. yield, resolution vs. burden, throughput vs. reliability, fixed vs. flexible cost, and velocity vs. privacy). However, a critical missing dimension is participant retention and dropout risk. The plan assumes 70–80% event-yield during 2–8 week stays, but provides no explicit lever controlling attrition due to anxiety, insomnia triggered by the facility environment, or protocol fatigue. Currently, extended stays and flexible staffing may exacerbate psychological strain if participants perceive slow event capture or staff inconsistency. Concrete addition: Insert a sixth lever titled 'Participant Support and Retention Architecture' with three options contrasting minimal clinical oversight (baseline protocol) versus integrated sleep psychologist interventions (cognitive therapy, sleep restriction, relaxation training during stay) versus external support pathway (daytime social engagement, family visit allowance, exit interview with teardown interviews to identify dropout predictors). This lens would rebalance the unit toward sustainability and data quality.

responses.strategic_rationale: This residential parasomnia research unit navigates three core tensions: (1) **Naturalism vs. Control**—a clinical environment suppresses genuine parasomnia expression, yet uncontrolled home settings lack safety and physiological instrumentation; (2) **Event Rarity vs. Data Efficiency**—infrequent episodes demand extended longitudinal stays and continuous monitoring, but long admissions strain budget and participant recruitment; (3) **Methodological Rigor vs. Operational Sustainability**—dual-scored AASM annotation and multi-night PSG protocols ensure quality but create labor bottlenecks that threaten the 50–70 participant enrollment target. The five levers below address these tensions by controlling: facility environment design (naturalism anchor), monitoring intensity scheduling (efficiency tuning), participant selection stringency (event-yield optimization), staffing and annotation workflow (sustainability), and data governance scope (publication velocity and reuse potential). Together, they determine whether the unit becomes a reproducible phenotyping platform or collapses under annotation burden and recruitment shortfall.

levers.name: Environment Authenticity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Design choice between clinical-grade safety (higher alarm false positives, suppressed behavior) vs. domestic aesthetics (lower annotation noise, naturalistic episodes) → Systemic: Hospital-converted suites yield 40% more captured parasomnia events but require additional 2–3 FTE technician time for false-alarm triage; residential conversion trades event quantity for 15% reduction in staffing overhead and faster participant retention → Strategic: Determines achievability of 70–80% event-capture rate and whether 10-week stay ceiling is tenable without budget overrun.
options: Retain full clinical safety apparatus (padded edges, restricted windows, bed rails, continuous video coverage, prominent sensors) accepting suppressed naturalistic behavior and higher technician time for false-positive screening

Adopt semi-concealed safety (discrete edge padding, electromagnetic locks on windows hidden in frames, infrared cameras disguised as artwork, microphones embedded in wallpapers) preserving visual domesticity while maintaining equipment fidelity

Implement tiered environments with two suites designed as certified sleep clinics for high-risk participants and six suites as furnished apartments with only essential sensors (mattress contact, dry EEG, concealed motion detection) for low-risk participants, enabling environment-matched recruitment
levers.review_lever: Controls Authenticity vs. Safety. Weakness: The options do not explicitly address how family members or bed partners visiting the facility might interfere with sensor integrity or event interpretation, nor do they specify how concealment affects informed consent transparency—a domestic-appearance suite with hidden microphones creates ethical friction that could expose the protocol to external review challenge.

levers.name: Monitoring Intensity Scheduling
levers.consequences: Immediate: Continuous full-PSG deployment requires 3 dedicated technicians per night and €180K/year in enhanced electrode/sensor consumables → Systemic: Scheduled enhanced nights (first, mid-stay, final) reduce consumable cost to €60K/year but risk missing 30% of events occurring on low-burden nights, requiring stay extensions and reducing throughput to 35–40 participants annually → Strategic: Determines cost-per-captured-event (€5K–€12K range) and whether the €1.2M year-two operations budget accommodates 30–35 enrollment target without quality compromise.
options: Deploy full clinical-grade PSG montage nightly for all participants, enabling autonomous capture and annotation workflow but consuming €180K/year in electrodes, amplifier time, and technician labor

Schedule enhanced PSG on first, mid-stay, and final nights per participant, supplemented by dry-EEG and contact-free sensors nightly; escalate to full PSG only on technician-flagged event nights, balancing cost (€60K/year consumables) against risk of unobserved episodes

Adopt high-resolution dry-electrode headband EEG with synchronized mattress ballisotcardiography and infrared motion capture as the routine baseline; reserve full PSG for a random 10% subsample of nights across all participants and all flagged event nights, enabling rapid event-yield assessment by week two with decision point for protocol adjustment.
levers.review_lever: Controls Cost vs. Event Capture Sensitivity. Weakness: None of the options quantifies the sensitivity/specificity trade-off explicitly—the escalation strategy assumes technicians reliably flag events in real time, but the plan provides no benchmark for inter-rater technician consistency or false-negative detection rates on low-burden nights, creating hidden risk that 'escalation nights' may miss slow, subtle confusional arousals that require full EMG resolution to classify.

levers.name: Participant Selection Stringency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict pre-admission PSG requirement (documented parasomnia on screening study) increases recruitment burden by 8–12 weeks per participant and eliminates 20% of symptomatic candidates who lack prior sleep studies → Systemic: Higher screening rigor yields 85% event-capture rate but reduces recruitment speed to 8–10 participants/year, extending the 50–70 enrollment window from 3 years to 5–7 years; relaxed criteria (clinical history + bed-partner collateral only) enable 25–30 participants/year but drop event yield to 65%, necessitating 12-week extensions for 15–20% of cohort, inflating stay costs by €240K/year → Strategic: Directly determines whether the 3-year grant window achieves the 50–70 target or requires no-cost extension, funding bridge, or acceptance of smaller final N.
options: Require pre-admission polysomnography confirming parasomnia on at least one captured cycle, supplemented by clinical interview and bed-partner collateral; exclude candidates with prior single-night study showing no events (assumes one-night PSG captures parasomnias if present)

Admit based on structured clinical interview by board-certified sleep physician, documented bed-partner or household collateral account of ≥2 monthly episodes with specific morphological features, and normal or unremarkable screening actigraphy; reserve PSG for in-unit baseline only, accelerating recruitment but accepting 20% diagnostic uncertainty on admission

Implement a two-tier recruitment pipeline: tier-one (prior PSG-confirmed) fast-tracked to 2-week baseline stays; tier-two (clinical history + collateral only) admitted to 4-week baseline stays with first-week full PSG to confirm diagnosis in-residence, enabling simultaneous recruitment expansion and event-yield hedging.
levers.review_lever: Controls Recruitment Speed vs. Event Certainty. Weakness: All three options anchor admission to self-reported episode frequency (≥2/month) but ignore the fact that participants with frequent parasomnias are more likely to have been diagnosed/treated already, potentially creating survivorship bias—candidates meeting frequency thresholds may disproportionately represent medication-refractory or high-severity phenotypes, limiting generalizability to the broader NREM parasomnia population.

levers.name: Annotation and Scoring Workflow Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Dual-scored AASM annotation by independent human raters requires €320K/year in external scoring contractor fees and 6-month turnaround on event analysis, delaying publication and Aim 3 algorithm development → Systemic: Real-time technician annotation plus single independent scorer reduces cost to €120K/year and enables monthly event-dataset refresh for algorithm training, but inter-rater reliability reporting drops from kappa=0.85 to κ=0.72, weakening peer-review acceptance and grant-renewal credibility → Strategic: Determines publication timeline (12 months vs. 24 months to first methods paper) and algorithm-development feasibility; cost savings free €200K/year for Aim 3 computational resources or participant enrollment acceleration.
options: Maintain dual-independent scoring by external sleep medicine specialists (€320K/year) with technician real-time annotation as triage input only; prioritize inter-rater reliability reporting and blinded re-scoring of disagreement cases, accepting 6-month annotation lag but securing 'gold-standard' event labels for algorithm benchmarking.

Implement real-time technician annotation (night staff who complete AASM training module and pass competency exam annually) immediately after event, followed by single independent expert scorer review and adjudication; reduce annotation cost to €120K/year and enable monthly dataset releases for algorithm iteration, reporting single-scorer accuracy against technician-flagged events.

Adopt a three-tier triage system: technician real-time flag and preliminary classification (5-minute post-event), semi-automated pre-scoring using rule-based movement/EEG thresholds to assign probability bins (parasomnia-likely, ambiguous, artifact-likely), and independent expert review of ambiguous and parasomnia-likely events only (40% of total flagged events), reducing scorer workload by 60% and cost to €90K/year while preserving blinded review for validation subset.
levers.review_lever: Controls Scoring Cost vs. Methodological Rigor. Weakness: The three-tier triage system (option 3) introduces a hidden dependency on rule-based pre-scoring accuracy that is never directly benchmarked—if the preliminary classification misses early-stage confusional arousals that lack prominent movement artifacts, the 'expert review only' pathway may systematically under-sample subtle phenotypes, biasing Aim 2 characterization toward high-amplitude, easily flagged events and obscuring clinical heterogeneity.

levers.name: Data Governance and Sharing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public repository deposition (physiological + de-identified sensor data, full BIDS compliance) requires 4–6 month post-study anonymization and ethics-amendment process, delayed first publication by 3 months, but attracts secondary research interest and positions facility as a resource platform → Systemic: Restricted-access model (data use agreements, firewall deposit in institutional repository) enables faster publication (3-month advantage) and protects privacy of identifiable features in video/audio, but limits secondary-use discovery and reduces long-term impact citation trajectory by 35–40% in meta-research studies → Strategic: Determines whether the unit establishes itself as a reproducible open-data foundation for parasomnia research (long-term funding sustainability, future collaboration opportunities) or prioritizes rapid publication velocity and grant-renewal evidence (near-term metrics for DFG renewal review at month 30).
options: Deposit de-identified physiological time-series (EEG, EOG, EMG, contact-free sensors, actigraphy) and event-annotated summaries in a public BIDS-compatible repository (OpenNeuro or equivalent) under CC-BY license with a 12-month embargo post-publication; restrict video and raw audio to institutional access under individual data use agreements; maximize research transparency and secondary-use potential.

Maintain all data (physiological, sensor, video, audio, event annotations) in a university-hosted institutional repository accessible only via formal data use agreement and ethics-board approval of secondary studies; publish methods and summary statistics openly but not raw datasets, preserving participant privacy and institutional control while enabling external algorithm benchmarking against de-identified summary tables.

Release a 'lite' BIDS dataset containing non-identifiable physiological variables (EEG spectral features, arousal indices, movement amplitude distributions) and aggregated event phenotypes (frequency, duration, temporal clustering) as public summary data; maintain a restricted-access 'full' dataset (raw signals, video, expert annotations) for qualified research teams, tiering access by institutional reputation and study design rigor to balance openness with oversight.
levers.review_lever: Controls Open Science vs. Privacy Protection. Weakness: All three options assume that de-identification of physiological data is sufficient to protect privacy, but wearable-era research has shown that high-frequency EEG or movement signatures can re-identify individuals when combined with demographic metadata; none of the options explicitly addresses whether re-identification risk through voice analysis (from embedded microphones), gait signature (from pressure-mat sensors), or temporal clustering patterns (admission dates + episode frequency) warrants additional de-identification techniques or stricter access tiers.

responses.summary: These five levers are well-aligned to the project's core tensions (naturalism vs. control, rarity vs. efficiency, rigor vs. sustainability), and they span orthogonal decision axes: environment (physical design), monitoring (temporal intensity), recruitment (participant criteria), workflow (labor/cost), and governance (publication speed). However, **one critical missing dimension is risk and contingency calibration**: the levers assume baseline success (70–80% event capture, 50–70 enrollment, 24-month operations survival) but do not expose decision branches for realistic failure modes. For instance, if event-yield falls below 40% by month 12, do you pivot to shorter stays and faster turnover, shift to more severe phenotypes (high-frequency parasomnias), or extend the facility lease and timeline? Add a **sixth conceptual lever—

responses.strategic_rationale: This parasomnia research facility navigates three fundamental tensions: (1) **Naturalism vs. Clinical Control**—domestic environment suppresses artifactual behavior, yet clinical-grade capture requires PSG infrastructure; (2) **Event Rarity vs. Data Efficiency**—infrequent episodes demand extended residential stays and tiered sensing, but prolonged monitoring strains staffing and participant burden; (3) **Safety vs. Operational Scalability**—intimate staff-participant ratios enable event response and consent integrity, yet limit participant throughput and profitability. The five levers below address these core conflicts by controlling recruitment inclusivity, sensing architecture, stay duration policy, staff deployment, and data governance—domains where distinct choices cascade through facility viability, scientific yield, and sustainable funding.

levers.name: Participant Phenotype Inclusivity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict exclusion (epilepsy, untreated apnea) vs. pragmatic inclusion (mild treated apnea, stable psychiatric medication) shifts recruitment pool by 30–40% and shortens pre-screening time from 6 weeks to 2 weeks → Systemic: Relaxed criteria increase event-yield variance and inter-rater scoring complexity by ~15%, requiring deeper dual-coder training and borderline-case adjudication panels → Strategic: High-specificity cohort produces clean phenotyping publications but takes 18+ months to enroll 50; inclusive cohort reaches 50 by month 12 but demands more nuanced comorbidity subgroup analyses and increases publication timeline for robust methods.
options: Strict clinical homogeneity: admit only pure NREM parasomniacs with no comorbid sleep or neurologic disorder; require at least two years seizure-free documented history and apnea-hypopnea index <5 on recent PSG

Pragmatic clinical balance: admit adults with NREM parasomnia plus stable treated conditions (controlled hypertension, anxiety on stable SSRI, mild obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP ≥5 nights/week with AHI <15 on therapy); require seizure freedom >3 years and capacity to consent independently

Transdiagnostic phenotype diversity: recruit NREM parasomniacs across the full spectrum of neuropsychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, prior substance use disorder in remission >6 months, mild untreated sleep apnea if participant-aware); stratify analyses by comorbidity subgroup and position the cohort as naturalistic population-based representation
levers.review_lever: Controls **Scientific Purity vs. Recruitment Speed**. Weakness: The options fail to address how to operationalize 'stable treated' conditions—no clear biomarker thresholds for medication adherence or apnea severity control during the residential stay, leaving scoring teams vulnerable to post-hoc uncertainty about whether a captured event represents parasomnia or sleep-apnea-triggered arousal.

levers.name: Tiered Sensing Architecture and Escalation Depth
levers.consequences: Immediate: Basal nightly dry-electrode EEG + mattress + infrared provides low false-alarm rate (~8%) and reduces technician annotation load by 35% vs. continuous PSG → Systemic: Scheduled enhanced PSG on days 1, mid-stay, final captures ~60–70% of infrequent events while full escalation PSG on technician-flagged nights adds 15–20% additional capture at cost of 40–50 extra PSG setups per 30-participant cohort, straining equipment availability and scoring bandwidth → Strategic: Lean tiered model scales to 12 suites with current staff and preserves participant comfort; aggressive full-escalation escalates costs by €120K–€180K annually and requires hiring a fourth technician, reducing year-one budget flexibility.
options: Conservative basal model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode headband, mattress pressure sensor, and infrared camera; reserve full PSG for days 1, mid-stay, and final only; technician escalation PSG triggered only after two independently scored events meet strict morphology criteria, reducing escalation PSG frequency to <5 per cohort

Balanced tiered-plus-threshold: continuous low-burden basal monitoring; scheduled enhanced PSG on days 1, mid-stay, final as planned; pragmatic escalation PSG deployment on nights where technician observes high-confidence motor behavior matching parasomnia phenotype (e.g., sustained leg movement ≥45 seconds) in real-time video review, targeting 8–12 escalation PSGs per cohort

Aggressive comprehensive capture: nightly low-burden basal plus scheduled PSG on days 1, 3, mid-stay, and final; full PSG deployment on any night with technician-flagged movement, arousal cluster, or unusual vital-sign pattern (heart-rate spike without EEG arousal marker); target 70–80% nights with PSG montage, enabling maximum morphology detail but requiring dedicated PSG technician hire and €180K annual equipment/staffing add.
levers.review_lever: Controls **Data Density vs. Operational Burden**. Weakness: The options do not specify how to validate technician real-time decision-making for escalation PSG deployment—no inter-technician reliability metric for 'high-confidence motor behavior' annotation, risking inconsistent escalation across night shifts and undermining reproducibility of event yield across the cohort.

levers.name: Participant Stay Duration and Admission Ceiling Policy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standard 2-week cap with optional 2-week extension for zero-event participants achieves 70–80% event yield in line with inclusion criterion (≥2 episodes/month) and keeps per-participant cost at ~€8K → Systemic: Extended-stay cohort (up to 10 weeks) captures tail-end infrequent participants but consumes 25–30% more facility-days, triggering waitlist delays and reducing annual throughput from 35 to ~26 participants per year, while increasing dropout risk as longer isolation raises participant burden → Strategic: Standard-cap model admits 50 participants by month 24 and publishes first longitudinal cohort at month 30; extended-stay model achieves higher per-participant event yield (77% vs. 80%) but enrollment curve flattens, delaying phase-2 grant positioning and requiring larger contingency budget for unpredictable demand.
options: Fixed 2-week baseline with no extension: all participants admitted for exactly 14 calendar days regardless of event capture; zero-event participants exit after 14 days and receive post-hoc contact tracing via 2-week actigraphy + monthly phone interview; maintains predictable facility occupancy and per-participant cost, achieving 70% event yield and supporting 34–36 annual admissions

Standard 2-week baseline plus extended optional pathway: participants receive scheduled 14-day stay; if zero adjudicated events after 14 days, offer optional 2-week extension (days 15–28); accept extensions on rolling basis dependent on facility occupancy; expected 35% take-up, increasing mean stay from 14 to 16.2 days and reducing annual throughput by ~8%, achieving 75% event yield per protocol specification

Adaptive stay length with participant co-design: baseline 10–14 days determined at intake based on reported episode frequency and technician confidence in event morphology after 5 days; low-confidence zero-event participants offered rolling 1-week extensions up to 10 weeks maximum with weekly collaborative review of data quality and participant well-being; prioritizes high-yield candidates and honors parasomnia variability but introduces administrative complexity (weekly consent reviews) and occupancy unpredictability.
levers.review_lever: Controls **Event Yield vs. Throughput Efficiency**. Weakness: None of the options address how to ethically manage participant expectation drift during extended stays—a zero-event participant on day 21 may experience increased anxiety or frustration, triggering behavioral changes (hyperarousal, sleep suppression) that paradoxically reduce event probability and confound longitudinal phenotyping aims.

levers.name: Night Technician Staffing Model and Incident Response Capacity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Three technicians rotating across 8 suites at 3 technicians-per-night (one technician per 2.7 suites on average) with a callout safety net achieves response time <3 minutes to any participant distress and enables real-time annotation → Systemic: Expansion to 12 suites with three technicians degrades response ratio to 1 technician per 4 suites, increasing false-alarm response delay to 5–7 minutes and annotation throughput loss during incident handling; hiring a fourth technician adds €85K annually (salary + social insurance) but restores margin and enables escalation PSG setup during active event monitoring → Strategic: Current 3-technician model locks facility capacity at 8 suites and year-3 expansion contingency to 12 suites unless fourth technician budget is committed by month 9; investing in technician hire at year 1 costs €85K upfront but enables doubling throughput in year 3 and supports larger phase-2 grant; deferring hire to year 2 preserves year-1 budget flexibility but sacrifices expansion optionality.
options: Lean core team: maintain 3 technicians covering 8-suite facility for all three years; absorb response-time trade-off of 4–5 minutes for any incident; explicitly cap facility expansion at 8 suites in protocol and defer phase-2 scaling to future grant; reduce annual operating cost by €85K and accept lower per-suite margin but preserve contingency for unexpected regulatory requirements or equipment failure

Standard-plus fourth technician: hire fourth technician in month 3 of year 1 at €85K annual cost (salary + benefits); maintain 1.3 technicians per suite average across 8 suites year 1–2; enable cross-training, escalation PSG setup efficiency, and smooth transition to 10–12-suite expansion in year 3; front-load personnel cost into year 1–2 budget but create flexibility for responsive scaling in response to enrollment velocity and event-capture performance

Dynamic staffing with per-protocol thresholds: operate with 3 technicians year 1 and commit to hiring fourth technician only if enrollment rate exceeds 18 participants in first 12 months or escalation PSG frequency exceeds 6 per month by month 6; use interim months to pilot flexible on-call scheduling with a trained research coordinator to absorb incident response; minimizes year-1 cost while maintaining go/no-go decision point at month 6.
levers.review_lever: Controls **Safety Margin vs. Budget Preservation**. Weakness: The options neglect technician burnout risk and its downstream impact on data quality—no mention of mandatory rest periods, peer-supervision models, or psychological decompression protocols after high-stress incident nights, which could degrade annotation accuracy and increase turnover in years 2–3.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development and Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Human-only dual-scoring (no automation) at launch produces gold-standard annotations for 60–80 parasomnia events by month 12, with inter-rater reliability (Cohen's κ, Krippendorff α) published transparently → Systemic: Investment of ~200–250 technician hours in independent scoring per 30-participant cohort yields high-confidence training labels; deploying a semi-automated triage tool (rule-based heuristics or supervised classifier) in year 2 against this gold standard reduces technician review load by 30–40% per event cluster but introduces 8–12% false-positive rate requiring human override, net reduction of 20–25% overall annotation workload → Strategic: Human-only pathway maintains scientific credibility and avoids overstated claims of autonomous detection, but limits year-2 and year-3 productivity gain and stretches publication timeline; algorithm-augmented pathway accelerates data throughput and supports larger phase-2 cohort designs but requires explicit disclosure of error rates and human-in-loop design in all publications.
options: Human consensus-only approach: all events scored independently by two trained raters using AASM criteria throughout all three years; no semi-automated triage tool development; inter-rater disagreements adjudicated by PI consensus; accept 15–20% longer annotation timelines and position the longitudinal dataset as highest-confidence reference standard for future algorithm validation by external teams

Semi-automated triage with supervised-learning classifier: develop a machine-learning classifier (random forest or gradient boosting) on year-1–2 gold-standard dual-scored events (~80–120 labeled parasomnia and control night clusters); deploy classifier in year 2 as first-pass screening to rank video segments and sensor clusters by parasomnia probability; human raters then focus depth scoring on high-confidence and borderline-score segments; report classifier sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against human consensus; explicitly frame tool as technician aid, not autonomous diagnosis

Real-time lightweight heuristic triage with continuous retraining: encode transparent rule-based heuristics (e.g., leg-EMG amplitude >100 µV + chin-EMG atonia + video motion >2 body lengths in <30 sec = high-probability parasomnia flag) into the real-time monitoring dashboard by month 6 year 1; refine thresholds monthly as dual-human scores accumulate; technician uses heuristic ranking in real-time to prioritize escalation PSG deployment; update rules quarterly with new data; maintain interpretability and avoid black-box claims, but accept slower algorithm evolution and potential threshold-tuning overfitting to early cohort quirks.
levers.review_lever: Controls **Throughput Automation vs. Scientific Transparency and Credibility**. Weakness: All three options underspecify how to handle data leakage—if the semi-automated classifier is trained and tuned on the same participants whose events inform longitudinal phenotyping results (Aim 2), the reported event frequencies and cluster patterns may be inflated or biased by the tool's systematic false-positive errors, violating statistical independence and undermining Aim 2 validity.

responses.summary: These five levers are well-distributed across recruitment, operations, staffing, and methodology—they collectively govern the core trade-offs between naturalism and rigor, event yield and efficiency, and safety and scalability. **Strengths**: Levers 1 and 3 directly address event-yield risk (Aim 2 phenotyping bottleneck); Lever 4 unlocks expansion optionality without forced overcommitment; Lever 5 guards against overselling automation to funders and journals. **Weaknesses**: (1) **Missing dimension—infrastructure and vendor lock-in risk**: No lever addresses equipment obsolescence, vendor dependency (e.g., if the dry-electrode headband manufacturer discontinues the product by year 2), or IT infrastructure resilience; a sixth lever on

levers.name: Participant Length-of-Stay Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Short stays (2 weeks) reduce per-participant cost and increase recruitment throughput, allowing 70+ enrollments in year one. Systemic: Extended stays (8+ weeks) capture lower-frequency events with 25–30% higher yield per participant but lock capital in fewer, longer admissions. Strategic: The choice determines whether the unit achieves Aim 2 longitudinal characterization via dense within-person sampling or via broader between-person heterogeneity mapping—with implications for publication timeline and algorithm generalizability.
options: Standardized 2-week stays for all participants regardless of event yield, with extension-on-demand only after zero-event protocol trigger, prioritizing recruitment volume and rapid turnover to reach 60+ enrollments by month 24
Variable stays structured as 4-week baseline for 80% of cohort plus 6–8 week extended protocols for participants with low event frequency or complex phenotypes, balancing yield and throughput

Adaptive-extension model: all participants default to 3-week stays; those with zero events by day 21 automatically extend to week 7 or 8 at reduced per-diem; those with ≥2 events exit after week 2 if participant-requested, preserving data while minimizing underutilized stays
levers.review: Controls Recruitment Volume vs. Event Yield. Weakness: the options do not explicitly address the staffing surge required during cohort turnover (intake/discharge cycles every 2 weeks in the short-stay model), which may compromise safety oversight and annotation quality during peak census transitions.

levers.name: Sensor-Tiering and Data-Burden Trade-off
levers.consequences: Immediate: Low-burden nightly dry-EEG headbands and mattress sensors enable high compliance and naturalistic behavior preservation, capturing 90%+ of nights. Systemic: Reduced overnight technician time for event response, but contact-free modalities suffer 15–20% false-positive artifact rates requiring higher manual-review burden. Strategic: Escalation-night full PSG adds clinical-grade resolution at ~€400 per night and delays clinical decision-making; omitting it risks missing rare morphological details required for Aim 3 algorithm training.
options: Lean tiering: nightly dry-EEG and contactless sensors only, with no scheduled enhanced-night PSG; full PSG deployed solely on nights after technician-flagged events, reducing equipment burden and freeing ~€150K year-one capital but risking sparse high-resolution data for algorithm development

Standard three-tier model (nightly lightweight + scheduled enhanced PSG on nights 1, mid-stay, final) plus event-triggered escalation, providing balanced training data at planned cost, with trade-off of participant discomfort and technician setup time on enhanced nights

Symmetric full-tier model: continuous full PSG (scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, leg EMG) on all nights alongside contactless sensors, maximizing artifact detection and morphological granularity but increasing equipment cost by ~€200K year one, technician load, and participant burden to the point of suppressing naturalistic behavior
levers.review: Controls Physiological Resolution vs. Operational Load. Weakness: none of the options address the unequal value of scheduled enhanced nights across participants—some may exhibit all events on odd nights, rendering mid-stay PSG non-contributory while missing critical episodes elsewhere.

levers.name: Event-Triage and Annotation Workflow Ownership
levers.consequences: Immediate: Technician real-time annotation during night shifts captures context and behavioral nuance but introduces fatigue bias and inconsistency. Systemic: Daytime dual-rater scoring ensures inter-rater reliability but 6–10 week annotation lag creates feedback delay to the technician, reducing quality control loop. Strategic: Automated pre-triage reduces manual burden by 30–40% but requires large training dataset (>500 events), pushing model maturity to month 20+, potentially delaying Aim 3 delivery.
options: Technician-centric: all real-time and next-day preliminary annotation performed by night technician and next-shift reviewer; daytime dual-rater scoring applied only to events flagged as borderline or novel, reducing dual-rater load by ~40% but risking systematic bias in what reaches expert review

Hybrid queue-based triage: technician flags potential events in real time; a statistical filter (e.g., movement amplitude + EEG arousal pattern thresholds) ranks flagged events by probability; daytime raters prioritize high-probability queue first, then exhaustively score lower-probability backlog, creating productivity tiering

Delayed-batch with algorithmic pre-screen: technician monitors safety only; all annotation deferred to daytime; candidate events automatically rank-ordered by a supervised-learning model trained on 100+ initial dual-scored nights; raters review model-ranked queue in priority order, completing Aim 3 algorithm concurrently with annotation
levers.review: Controls Annotation-Throughput vs. Inter-Rater Reliability. Weakness: the options assume stable technician roster and dual-rater availability, but staff turnover (especially night technicians) will degrade real-time tagging consistency and daytime rater expertise unevenly across the 3-year period, yet none offer mitigation or recalibration protocols.

levers.name: Staffing Flexibility and Night-Coverage Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Fixed 3-technician roster covers 8 suites safely but offers no surge capacity when census approaches 12 suites or when technician absence occurs. Systemic: On-call rotation plus one additional part-time technician adds ~€80K year-two salary but enables 10–12 suite operation and buffers staff turnover; alternatively, outsourced overnight staffing (temp agency) reduces fixed cost but introduces inconsistency and delayed response. Strategic: Staffing determines maximum safe census, directly constraining enrollment rate and event yield; undersizing creates bottlenecks; oversizing in year one wastes budget before data quality is proven.
options: Core fixed roster: retain planned 3 full-time technicians for 8 active suites with strict no-flex admissions ceiling; defer 9–12 suite staffing to phase two; saves ~€200K year one but caps annual enrollment at ~35 participants and risks project failure if event yield is lower than predicted

Tiered flexible model: 3 core FTE technicians for 8 suites baseline; hire one additional 0.5 FTE night technician in year two (shared with daytime annotation) to enable 10-suite operation; implement structured on-call for emergencies without planned reliance, increasing flexibility while controlling cost escalation

Hybrid outsource-and-in-house: retain 2 core in-house technicians for continuity and training; contract external night-shift staffing for 50% of coverage (e.g., 3–4 nights per week from a vetted clinical staffing agency), reducing fixed overhead by ~€150K annually but risking protocol-adherence gaps and increased incident reporting burden
levers.review: Controls Fixed Cost vs. Adaptive Capacity. Weakness: the options ignore the training and onboarding burden for external or part-time staff—overnight technicians require 4–6 weeks of competency building on the tiered sensor suite and safety protocols, yet none of the models front-load this time or cost explicitly.

levers.name: Data-Sharing Scope and Publication-Acceleration Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: De-identified physiological-only repository deposits (excluding video) enable rapid public archiving and third-party algorithm benchmarking by month 18, accelerating external validation and citation impact. Systemic: Limiting public data to sensor streams and scored-event annotations (not raw video) protects privacy but excludes qualitative behavioral context that enriches phenotyping; restricting sharing to signed data-use agreements delays deposit timeline by 6–12 weeks per request. Strategic: Delaying public data release until final report (month 36) maximizes the unit's publication window but risks parallel external work and reduces early impact; releasing mid-stream (month 18+) seeds the field but may expose methodological limitations before peer-review publication.
options: Early open-access deposit: physiological data and event annotations archived in a public BIDS-compliant repository (e.g., OpenNeuro) by month 16, concurrent with first-draft manuscript submission; video excluded; minimal data-use agreement overhead; prioritizes rapid field engagement and algorithm replication over proprietary advantage

Tiered staggered release: first interim dataset (cohort 1, n=25) with 12-month embargo under institutional control, released with accompanying methods paper at month 20; second dataset (cohort 2, n=25–40) at month 28; final cohort released month 36; balances publication priority with controlled dissemination and partner influence windows

Restricted access with consortium: physiological data available only to investigator-nominated collaborators under formal data-use agreements reviewed by PI and advisory board; video never shared; full dataset archive restricted to qualified sleep research institutions; maximizes confidentiality and negotiating leverage with pharmaceutical partners but delays external validation and risks replication challenges
levers.review: Controls Publication Velocity vs. Privacy and Partnership Leverage. Weakness: the options treat video as uniformly excludable, yet some de-identified lower-body or hands-only video of motor phenomena (absent faces) could enrich phenotyping and algorithm training without severe privacy violation; none of the options explore granular video-segmentation or temporal-blur approaches that might unlock conditional sharing.

levers.name: Environment Authenticity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Design choice between clinical-grade safety (higher alarm false positives, suppressed behavior) vs. domestic aesthetics (lower annotation noise, naturalistic episodes) → Systemic: Hospital-converted suites yield 40% more captured parasomnia events but require additional 2–3 FTE technician time for false-alarm triage; residential conversion trades event quantity for 15% reduction in staffing overhead and faster participant retention → Strategic: Determines achievability of 70–80% event-capture rate and whether 10-week stay ceiling is tenable without budget overrun.
options: Retain full clinical safety apparatus (padded edges, restricted windows, bed rails, continuous video coverage, prominent sensors) accepting suppressed naturalistic behavior and higher technician time for false-positive screening

Adopt semi-concealed safety (discrete edge padding, electromagnetic locks on windows hidden in frames, infrared cameras disguised as artwork, microphones embedded in wallpapers) preserving visual domesticity while maintaining equipment fidelity

Implement tiered environments with two suites designed as certified sleep clinics for high-risk participants and six suites as furnished apartments with only essential sensors (mattress contact, dry EEG, concealed motion detection) for low-risk participants, enabling environment-matched recruitment
levers.review: Controls Authenticity vs. Safety. Weakness: The options do not explicitly address how family members or bed partners visiting the facility might interfere with sensor integrity or event interpretation, nor do they specify how concealment affects informed consent transparency—a domestic-appearance suite with hidden microphones creates ethical friction that could expose the protocol to external review challenge.

levers.name: Monitoring Intensity Scheduling
levers.consequences: Immediate: Continuous full-PSG deployment requires 3 dedicated technicians per night and €180K/year in enhanced electrode/sensor consumables → Systemic: Scheduled enhanced nights (first, mid-stay, final) reduce consumable cost to €60K/year but risk missing 30% of events occurring on low-burden nights, requiring stay extensions and reducing throughput to 35–40 participants annually → Strategic: Determines cost-per-captured-event (€5K–€12K range) and whether the €1.2M year-two operations budget accommodates 30–35 enrollment target without quality compromise.
options: Deploy full clinical-grade PSG montage nightly for all participants, enabling autonomous capture and annotation workflow but consuming €180K/year in electrodes, amplifier time, and technician labor

Schedule enhanced PSG on first, mid-stay, and final nights per participant, supplemented by dry-EEG and contact-free sensors nightly; escalate to full PSG only on technician-flagged event nights, balancing cost (€60K/year consumables) against risk of unobserved episodes

Adopt high-resolution dry-electrode headband EEG with synchronized mattress ballisotcardiography and infrared motion capture as the routine baseline; reserve full PSG for a random 10% subsample of nights across all participants and all flagged event nights, enabling rapid event-yield assessment by week two with decision point for protocol adjustment.
levers.review: Controls Cost vs. Event Capture Sensitivity. Weakness: None of the options quantifies the sensitivity/specificity trade-off explicitly—the escalation strategy assumes technicians reliably flag events in real time, but the plan provides no benchmark for inter-rater technician consistency or false-negative detection rates on low-burden nights, creating hidden risk that 'escalation nights' may miss slow, subtle confusional arousals that require full EMG resolution to classify.

levers.name: Participant Selection Stringency
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict pre-admission PSG requirement (documented parasomnia on screening study) increases recruitment burden by 8–12 weeks per participant and eliminates 20% of symptomatic candidates who lack prior sleep studies → Systemic: Higher screening rigor yields 85% event-capture rate but reduces recruitment speed to 8–10 participants/year, extending the 50–70 enrollment window from 3 years to 5–7 years; relaxed criteria (clinical history + bed-partner collateral only) enable 25–30 participants/year but drop event yield to 65%, necessitating 12-week extensions for 15–20% of cohort, inflating stay costs by €240K/year → Strategic: Directly determines whether the 3-year grant window achieves the 50–70 target or requires no-cost extension, funding bridge, or acceptance of smaller final N.
options: Require pre-admission polysomnography confirming parasomnia on at least one captured cycle, supplemented by clinical interview and bed-partner collateral; exclude candidates with prior single-night study showing no events (assumes one-night PSG captures parasomnias if present)

Admit based on structured clinical interview by board-certified sleep physician, documented bed-partner or household collateral account of ≥2 monthly episodes with specific morphological features, and normal or unremarkable screening actigraphy; reserve PSG for in-unit baseline only, accelerating recruitment but accepting 20% diagnostic uncertainty on admission

Implement a two-tier recruitment pipeline: tier-one (prior PSG-confirmed) fast-tracked to 2-week baseline stays; tier-two (clinical history + collateral only) admitted to 4-week baseline stays with first-week full PSG to confirm diagnosis in-residence, enabling simultaneous recruitment expansion and event-yield hedging.
levers.review: Controls Recruitment Speed vs. Event Certainty. Weakness: All three options anchor admission to self-reported episode frequency (≥2/month) but ignore the fact that participants with frequent parasomnias are more likely to have been diagnosed/treated already, potentially creating survivorship bias—candidates meeting frequency thresholds may disproportionately represent medication-refractory or high-severity phenotypes, limiting generalizability to the broader NREM parasomnia population.

levers.name: Annotation and Scoring Workflow Architecture
levers.consequences: Immediate: Dual-scored AASM annotation by independent human raters requires €320K/year in external scoring contractor fees and 6-month turnaround on event analysis, delaying publication and Aim 3 algorithm development → Systemic: Real-time technician annotation plus single independent scorer reduces cost to €120K/year and enables monthly event-dataset refresh for algorithm training, but inter-rater reliability reporting drops from kappa=0.85 to κ=0.72, weakening peer-review acceptance and grant-renewal credibility → Strategic: Determines publication timeline (12 months vs. 24 months to first methods paper) and algorithm-development feasibility; cost savings free €200K/year for Aim 3 computational resources or participant enrollment acceleration.
options: Maintain dual-independent scoring by external sleep medicine specialists (€320K/year) with technician real-time annotation as triage input only; prioritize inter-rater reliability reporting and blinded re-scoring of disagreement cases, accepting 6-month annotation lag but securing 'gold-standard' event labels for algorithm benchmarking.

Implement real-time technician annotation (night staff who complete AASM training module and pass competency exam annually) immediately after event, followed by single independent expert scorer review and adjudication; reduce annotation cost to €120K/year and enable monthly dataset releases for algorithm iteration, reporting single-scorer accuracy against technician-flagged events.

Adopt a three-tier triage system: technician real-time flag and preliminary classification (5-minute post-event), semi-automated pre-scoring using rule-based movement/EEG thresholds to assign probability bins (parasomnia-likely, ambiguous, artifact-likely), and independent expert review of ambiguous and parasomnia-likely events only (40% of total flagged events), reducing scorer workload by 60% and cost to €90K/year while preserving blinded review for validation subset.
levers.review: Controls Scoring Cost vs. Methodological Rigor. Weakness: The three-tier triage system (option 3) introduces a hidden dependency on rule-based pre-scoring accuracy that is never directly benchmarked—if the preliminary classification misses early-stage confusional arousals that lack prominent movement artifacts, the 'expert review only' pathway may systematically under-sample subtle phenotypes, biasing Aim 2 characterization toward high-amplitude, easily flagged events and obscuring clinical heterogeneity.

levers.name: Data Governance and Sharing Model
levers.consequences: Immediate: Public repository deposition (physiological + de-identified sensor data, full BIDS compliance) requires 4–6 month post-study anonymization and ethics-amendment process, delayed first publication by 3 months, but attracts secondary research interest and positions facility as a resource platform → Systemic: Restricted-access model (data use agreements, firewall deposit in institutional repository) enables faster publication (3-month advantage) and protects privacy of identifiable features in video/audio, but limits secondary-use discovery and reduces long-term impact citation trajectory by 35–40% in meta-research studies → Strategic: Determines whether the unit establishes itself as a reproducible open-data foundation for parasomnia research (long-term funding sustainability, future collaboration opportunities) or prioritizes rapid publication velocity and grant-renewal evidence (near-term metrics for DFG renewal review at month 30).
options: Deposit de-identified physiological time-series (EEG, EOG, EMG, contact-free sensors, actigraphy) and event-annotated summaries in a public BIDS-compatible repository (OpenNeuro or equivalent) under CC-BY license with a 12-month embargo post-publication; restrict video and raw audio to institutional access under individual data use agreements; maximize research transparency and secondary-use potential.

Maintain all data (physiological, sensor, video, audio, event annotations) in a university-hosted institutional repository accessible only via formal data use agreement and ethics-board approval of secondary studies; publish methods and summary statistics openly but not raw datasets, preserving participant privacy and institutional control while enabling external algorithm benchmarking against de-identified summary tables.

Release a 'lite' BIDS dataset containing non-identifiable physiological variables (EEG spectral features, arousal indices, movement amplitude distributions) and aggregated event phenotypes (frequency, duration, temporal clustering) as public summary data; maintain a restricted-access 'full' dataset (raw signals, video, expert annotations) for qualified research teams, tiering access by institutional reputation and study design rigor to balance openness with oversight.
levers.review: Controls Open Science vs. Privacy Protection. Weakness: All three options assume that de-identification of physiological data is sufficient to protect privacy, but wearable-era research has shown that high-frequency EEG or movement signatures can re-identify individuals when combined with demographic metadata; none of the options explicitly addresses whether re-identification risk through voice analysis (from embedded microphones), gait signature (from pressure-mat sensors), or temporal clustering patterns (admission dates + episode frequency) warrants additional de-identification techniques or stricter access tiers.

levers.name: Participant Phenotype Inclusivity Strategy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Strict exclusion (epilepsy, untreated apnea) vs. pragmatic inclusion (mild treated apnea, stable psychiatric medication) shifts recruitment pool by 30–40% and shortens pre-screening time from 6 weeks to 2 weeks → Systemic: Relaxed criteria increase event-yield variance and inter-rater scoring complexity by ~15%, requiring deeper dual-coder training and borderline-case adjudication panels → Strategic: High-specificity cohort produces clean phenotyping publications but takes 18+ months to enroll 50; inclusive cohort reaches 50 by month 12 but demands more nuanced comorbidity subgroup analyses and increases publication timeline for robust methods.
options: Strict clinical homogeneity: admit only pure NREM parasomniacs with no comorbid sleep or neurologic disorder; require at least two years seizure-free documented history and apnea-hypopnea index <5 on recent PSG

Pragmatic clinical balance: admit adults with NREM parasomnia plus stable treated conditions (controlled hypertension, anxiety on stable SSRI, mild obstructive sleep apnea on CPAP ≥5 nights/week with AHI <15 on therapy); require seizure freedom >3 years and capacity to consent independently

Transdiagnostic phenotype diversity: recruit NREM parasomniacs across the full spectrum of neuropsychiatric comorbidity (depression, anxiety, prior substance use disorder in remission >6 months, mild untreated sleep apnea if participant-aware); stratify analyses by comorbidity subgroup and position the cohort as naturalistic population-based representation
levers.review: Controls **Scientific Purity vs. Recruitment Speed**. Weakness: The options fail to address how to operationalize 'stable treated' conditions—no clear biomarker thresholds for medication adherence or apnea severity control during the residential stay, leaving scoring teams vulnerable to post-hoc uncertainty about whether a captured event represents parasomnia or sleep-apnea-triggered arousal.

levers.name: Tiered Sensing Architecture and Escalation Depth
levers.consequences: Immediate: Basal nightly dry-electrode EEG + mattress + infrared provides low false-alarm rate (~8%) and reduces technician annotation load by 35% vs. continuous PSG → Systemic: Scheduled enhanced PSG on days 1, mid-stay, final captures ~60–70% of infrequent events while full escalation PSG on technician-flagged nights adds 15–20% additional capture at cost of 40–50 extra PSG setups per 30-participant cohort, straining equipment availability and scoring bandwidth → Strategic: Lean tiered model scales to 12 suites with current staff and preserves participant comfort; aggressive full-escalation escalates costs by €120K–€180K annually and requires hiring a fourth technician, reducing year-one budget flexibility.
options: Conservative basal model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode headband, mattress pressure sensor, and infrared camera; reserve full PSG for days 1, mid-stay, and final only; technician escalation PSG triggered only after two independently scored events meet strict morphology criteria, reducing escalation PSG frequency to <5 per cohort

Balanced tiered-plus-threshold: continuous low-burden basal monitoring; scheduled enhanced PSG on days 1, mid-stay, final as planned; pragmatic escalation PSG deployment on nights where technician observes high-confidence motor behavior matching parasomnia phenotype (e.g., sustained leg movement ≥45 seconds) in real-time video review, targeting 8–12 escalation PSGs per cohort

Aggressive comprehensive capture: nightly low-burden basal plus scheduled PSG on days 1, 3, mid-stay, and final; full PSG deployment on any night with technician-flagged movement, arousal cluster, or unusual vital-sign pattern (heart-rate spike without EEG arousal marker); target 70–80% nights with PSG montage, enabling maximum morphology detail but requiring dedicated PSG technician hire and €180K annual equipment/staffing add.
levers.review: Controls **Data Density vs. Operational Burden**. Weakness: The options do not specify how to validate technician real-time decision-making for escalation PSG deployment—no inter-technician reliability metric for 'high-confidence motor behavior' annotation, risking inconsistent escalation across night shifts and undermining reproducibility of event yield across the cohort.

levers.name: Participant Stay Duration and Admission Ceiling Policy
levers.consequences: Immediate: Standard 2-week cap with optional 2-week extension for zero-event participants achieves 70–80% event yield in line with inclusion criterion (≥2 episodes/month) and keeps per-participant cost at ~€8K → Systemic: Extended-stay cohort (up to 10 weeks) captures tail-end infrequent participants but consumes 25–30% more facility-days, triggering waitlist delays and reducing annual throughput from 35 to ~26 participants per year, while increasing dropout risk as longer isolation raises participant burden → Strategic: Standard-cap model admits 50 participants by month 24 and publishes first longitudinal cohort at month 30; extended-stay model achieves higher per-participant event yield (77% vs. 80%) but enrollment curve flattens, delaying phase-2 grant positioning and requiring larger contingency budget for unpredictable demand.
options: Fixed 2-week baseline with no extension: all participants admitted for exactly 14 calendar days regardless of event capture; zero-event participants exit after 14 days and receive post-hoc contact tracing via 2-week actigraphy + monthly phone interview; maintains predictable facility occupancy and per-participant cost, achieving 70% event yield and supporting 34–36 annual admissions

Standard 2-week baseline plus extended optional pathway: participants receive scheduled 14-day stay; if zero adjudicated events after 14 days, offer optional 2-week extension (days 15–28); accept extensions on rolling basis dependent on facility occupancy; expected 35% take-up, increasing mean stay from 14 to 16.2 days and reducing annual throughput by ~8%, achieving 75% event yield per protocol specification

Adaptive stay length with participant co-design: baseline 10–14 days determined at intake based on reported episode frequency and technician confidence in event morphology after 5 days; low-confidence zero-event participants offered rolling 1-week extensions up to 10 weeks maximum with weekly collaborative review of data quality and participant well-being; prioritizes high-yield candidates and honors parasomnia variability but introduces administrative complexity (weekly consent reviews) and occupancy unpredictability.
levers.review: Controls **Event Yield vs. Throughput Efficiency**. Weakness: None of the options address how to ethically manage participant expectation drift during extended stays—a zero-event participant on day 21 may experience increased anxiety or frustration, triggering behavioral changes (hyperarousal, sleep suppression) that paradoxically reduce event probability and confound longitudinal phenotyping aims.

levers.name: Night Technician Staffing Model and Incident Response Capacity
levers.consequences: Immediate: Three technicians rotating across 8 suites at 3 technicians-per-night (one technician per 2.7 suites on average) with a callout safety net achieves response time <3 minutes to any participant distress and enables real-time annotation → Systemic: Expansion to 12 suites with three technicians degrades response ratio to 1 technician per 4 suites, increasing false-alarm response delay to 5–7 minutes and annotation throughput loss during incident handling; hiring a fourth technician adds €85K annually (salary + social insurance) but restores margin and enables escalation PSG setup during active event monitoring → Strategic: Current 3-technician model locks facility capacity at 8 suites and year-3 expansion contingency to 12 suites unless fourth technician budget is committed by month 9; investing in technician hire at year 1 costs €85K upfront but enables doubling throughput in year 3 and supports larger phase-2 grant; deferring hire to year 2 preserves year-1 budget flexibility but sacrifices expansion optionality.
options: Lean core team: maintain 3 technicians covering 8-suite facility for all three years; absorb response-time trade-off of 4–5 minutes for any incident; explicitly cap facility expansion at 8 suites in protocol and defer phase-2 scaling to future grant; reduce annual operating cost by €85K and accept lower per-suite margin but preserve contingency for unexpected regulatory requirements or equipment failure

Standard-plus fourth technician: hire fourth technician in month 3 of year 1 at €85K annual cost (salary + benefits); maintain 1.3 technicians per suite average across 8 suites year 1–2; enable cross-training, escalation PSG setup efficiency, and smooth transition to 10–12-suite expansion in year 3; front-load personnel cost into year 1–2 budget but create flexibility for responsive scaling in response to enrollment velocity and event-capture performance

Dynamic staffing with per-protocol thresholds: operate with 3 technicians year 1 and commit to hiring fourth technician only if enrollment rate exceeds 18 participants in first 12 months or escalation PSG frequency exceeds 6 per month by month 6; use interim months to pilot flexible on-call scheduling with a trained research coordinator to absorb incident response; minimizes year-1 cost while maintaining go/no-go decision point at month 6.
levers.review: Controls **Safety Margin vs. Budget Preservation**. Weakness: The options neglect technician burnout risk and its downstream impact on data quality—no mention of mandatory rest periods, peer-supervision models, or psychological decompression protocols after high-stress incident nights, which could degrade annotation accuracy and increase turnover in years 2–3.

levers.name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development and Validation Pathway
levers.consequences: Immediate: Human-only dual-scoring (no automation) at launch produces gold-standard annotations for 60–80 parasomnia events by month 12, with inter-rater reliability (Cohen's κ, Krippendorff α) published transparently → Systemic: Investment of ~200–250 technician hours in independent scoring per 30-participant cohort yields high-confidence training labels; deploying a semi-automated triage tool (rule-based heuristics or supervised classifier) in year 2 against this gold standard reduces technician review load by 30–40% per event cluster but introduces 8–12% false-positive rate requiring human override, net reduction of 20–25% overall annotation workload → Strategic: Human-only pathway maintains scientific credibility and avoids overstated claims of autonomous detection, but limits year-2 and year-3 productivity gain and stretches publication timeline; algorithm-augmented pathway accelerates data throughput and supports larger phase-2 cohort designs but requires explicit disclosure of error rates and human-in-loop design in all publications.
options: Human consensus-only approach: all events scored independently by two trained raters using AASM criteria throughout all three years; no semi-automated triage tool development; inter-rater disagreements adjudicated by PI consensus; accept 15–20% longer annotation timelines and position the longitudinal dataset as highest-confidence reference standard for future algorithm validation by external teams

Semi-automated triage with supervised-learning classifier: develop a machine-learning classifier (random forest or gradient boosting) on year-1–2 gold-standard dual-scored events (~80–120 labeled parasomnia and control night clusters); deploy classifier in year 2 as first-pass screening to rank video segments and sensor clusters by parasomnia probability; human raters then focus depth scoring on high-confidence and borderline-score segments; report classifier sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against human consensus; explicitly frame tool as technician aid, not autonomous diagnosis

Real-time lightweight heuristic triage with continuous retraining: encode transparent rule-based heuristics (e.g., leg-EMG amplitude >100 µV + chin-EMG atonia + video motion >2 body lengths in <30 sec = high-probability parasomnia flag) into the real-time monitoring dashboard by month 6 year 1; refine thresholds monthly as dual-human scores accumulate; technician uses heuristic ranking in real-time to prioritize escalation PSG deployment; update rules quarterly with new data; maintain interpretability and avoid black-box claims, but accept slower algorithm evolution and potential threshold-tuning overfitting to early cohort quirks.
levers.review: Controls **Throughput Automation vs. Scientific Transparency and Credibility**. Weakness: All three options underspecify how to handle data leakage—if the semi-automated classifier is trained and tuned on the same participants whose events inform longitudinal phenotyping results (Aim 2), the reported event frequencies and cluster patterns may be inflated or biased by the tool's systematic false-positive errors, violating statistical independence and undermining Aim 2 validity.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate EXACTLY 5 levers per response. Do not generate more or fewer than 5 levers.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences MUST:
     • Chain three SPECIFIC effects: "Immediate: [effect] → Systemic: [impact] → Strategic: [implication]"
     • Include measurable outcomes: "Systemic: 25% faster scaling through..."
     • Explicitly describe trade-offs between core tensions
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional/innovative approach
     • Show clear progression: conservative → moderate → radical
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name levers as strategic concepts (e.g., "Material Adaptation Strategy")
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     • State the trade-off explicitly: "Controls [Tension A] vs. [Tension B]."
     • Identify a specific weakness: "Weakness: The options fail to consider [specific factor]."
   - For `summary`:
     • Identify ONE critical missing dimension
     • Prescribe CONCRETE addition: "Add '[full strategic option]' to [lever]"

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences
   - NO "[specific innovative option]" placeholders
   - NO value sets without clear strategic progression

6. **Option Structure Enforcement**
   - Radical option must include emerging tech/business model
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported.
